section: Why displays, sensors, and flash use SPI
text:
SPI shows up wherever a device wants a fast, cheap, point-to-point link and does not need many masters. Displays are a clear case: pushing a framebuffer is mostly one-directional, high-volume writes, and SPI's full-duplex clocking handles that at tens of megabits without protocol overhead. Many small panel controllers, including the ILI9341 class of TFT drivers, speak SPI with a separate data/command GPIO alongside the bus.

Sensors like it because a transaction is often just "send a register address, read back the value," which the full-duplex model does in one exchange. NOR flash uses SPI because the chip count is low and throughput matters; the dual and octal QSPI variants exist precisely to keep flash fast. The tradeoffs are honest. SPI has no built-in flow control, no error checking, and no standard for sharing the bus among multiple controllers. What it offers is determinism and speed on a short board-level link, which is exactly what these three device classes want.

text:
Linux splits SPI into two driver kinds. A controller driver manages the host hardware and exposes it as a struct spi_controller; a protocol driver speaks to one peripheral and registers as a struct spi_driver with probe() and remove() callbacks. Each peripheral on the bus is represented by a struct spi_device, the controller-side proxy that holds the mode, bits-per-word, and max clock speed for that part. Binding happens by matching: the kernel compares a device's modalias, usually filled from a device-tree compatible string or an of_device_id table, against registered drivers and calls probe() on a hit.

Data moves through two paired structures. A struct spi_transfer is one full-duplex segment with optional tx_buf and rx_buf pointers; a struct spi_message chains several transfers that run atomically so no other device interrupts the sequence. Drivers submit with spi_sync(), which blocks until done and is the common path, or spi_async(), which queues the message and fires a completion callback. The documentation has shifted from master/slave toward controller/peripheral (and controller/target) wording, though the older struct field names linger.

text:
For large transfers the controller can use DMA so the CPU does not copy every word. A controller driver advertises this through a can_dma() callback, and the SPI core maps the transfer buffers into tx_sg and rx_sg scatterlists for it. The catch is that DMA buffers must live in DMA-safe memory; a buffer on the kernel stack is not safe to hand to a DMA engine, so drivers allocate transfer buffers from an appropriate pool. For repeated identical messages, spi_optimize_message() does the validation and setup once and spi_unoptimize_message() releases it.

Rust-for-Linux is where this gets current. Rust support in the kernel was merged in v6.1, and at the 2025 Maintainers Summit the "Rust experiment" was formally declared a success and concluded, per reporting from Phoronix. Most Rust work lives in drivers and the abstraction layers that let a Rust driver call a C subsystem safely. A safe SPI abstraction has been worked on since at least Kangrejos 2021, originally targeting an arm64 device driver. As far as is documented in mid-2026, a fully merged in-tree kernel::spi abstraction with stable spi::Driver and spi::Device types is still in progress rather than settled mainline; the authoritative status lives on the rust-for-linux tree and the linux-spi list.

text: Warhammer 40,000 is a tabletop miniatures wargame published by Games Workshop. It first appeared in 1987 as Warhammer 40,000: Rogue Trader, and it has run through ten editions, with an eleventh arriving in June 2026. Players collect, assemble, and paint small models of soldiers, tanks, and monsters, then move them across a table and resolve battles with dice and a rulebook. Around that game sits one of the deepest fictional settings in science fiction, sustained by codex army books, novels, and video games. The official tagline reads: "In the grim darkness of the far future, there is only war." The phrase did real work; it gave English the word grimdark, now used well outside the hobby. The setting is roughly the year 40,000, the close of the 41st Millennium. It is deliberately hopeless. Reason has decayed into superstition, science into ritual, and the galaxy is host to daemons, mutation, and constant death. Rick Priestley, who built the original setting, has cited Tolkien, H. P. Lovecraft, Frank Herbert's Dune, Milton's Paradise Lost, and the British comic 2000 AD as influences.

text: The setting's central faction is the Imperium of Man, a galaxy-spanning human empire and a paranoid, authoritarian theocracy. It is ruled in name by the Emperor of Mankind, the most powerful human psyker ever born, who united Earth in the late 30th Millennium and set out to reclaim the galaxy for humanity. That project collapsed in civil war. Betrayed and mortally wounded by his favored son Horus during the Horus Heresy, the Emperor has spent more than ten thousand years interred in the Golden Throne, a life-support machine that keeps his soul anchored to a withered body and consumes a thousand psykers' souls a day. He is worshipped as a god. His will projects across the galaxy as the Astronomican, a psychic beacon by which ships navigate. His greatest soldiers are the Adeptus Astartes, the Space Marines, eight-foot bioengineered warriors grown from his genetic material. In the modern timeline a vast warp storm called the Great Rift has split the Imperium in two, and the resurrected primarch Roboute Guilliman now leads its defense.

text: The Warp, also called the Immaterium, is a parallel dimension of pure psychic energy that underlies all reality. Every emotion and thought of every sentient creature ripples into it. Over aeons those currents congealed into the Chaos Gods, the Ruinous Powers: Khorne, god of blood and rage; Tzeentch, of change and sorcery; Nurgle, of disease and decay; and Slaanesh, of excess. They are sustained by mortal feeling and so are utterly single-minded. They wage an eternal rivalry called the Great Game, and at times unite under Chaos Undivided to bring down the Imperium. Their servants are daemons, made from a fragment of a god's own power. The Warp matters twice over. It is the home of Chaos, and it is the only route between the stars, since ships translate into it to cross the galaxy. The Warp was not always cruel; aeons of war twisted a once-calmer realm into a nightmare. That souring is tied to the Necrons' own history.

section: The Necrontyr and the War in Heaven
text: Before the Necrons there were the Necrontyr, a flesh-and-blood species born under a sickly, radiation-spewing star. Their lives were short and cancer-ridden, and the whole culture grew obsessed with death and dynastic hierarchy. They built a vast star-empire, then tore at it through endless infighting. To unite the dynasties, their rulers found a common foe: the Old Ones, the galaxy's most ancient and powerful race, whose mastery of the Warp the Necrontyr envied and resented. The war they started, the War in Heaven, was one of the bloodiest in galactic history, fought roughly sixty million years before the 41st Millennium. It went catastrophically. The Old Ones' command of the Warp and their living weapons broke the Necrontyr again and again, driving them to the galaxy's fringe to nurse their hatred. There, exiled and desperate, they encountered beings of pure energy living on the surfaces of stars. The Necrontyr named them the C'tan, the star-gods, and made a bargain that would unmake them.
embedText:
Warhammer 40,000 and the Necrons — The Necrontyr and the War in Heaven

Before the Necrons there were the Necrontyr, a flesh-and-blood species born under a sickly, radiation-spewing star. Their lives were short and cancer-ridden, and the whole culture grew obsessed with death and dynastic hierarchy. They built a vast star-empire, then tore at it through endless infighting. To unite the dynasties, their rulers found a common foe: the Old Ones, the galaxy's most ancient and powerful race, whose mastery of the Warp the Necrontyr envied and resented. The war they started, the War in Heaven, was one of the bloodiest in galactic history, fought roughly sixty million years before the 41st Millennium. It went catastrophically. The Old Ones' command of the Warp and their living weapons broke the Necrontyr again and again, driving them to the galaxy's fringe to nurse their hatred. There, exiled and desperate, they encountered beings of pure energy living on the surfaces of stars. The Necrontyr named them the C'tan, the star-gods, and made a bargain that would unmake them.

text: The Necrontyr forged bodies of necrodermis, living metal, so the C'tan could take material form. Fed and embodied, the star-gods turned on their makers and terrorized them into service. Then the C'tan offered a final pact, championed by the one later called the Deceiver: physical immortality. Through biotransference, the Necrontyr would shed their dying flesh and have their minds poured into living-metal shells like the C'tan's own. Vast bio-foundries were built; the Cryptek Illuminor Szeras turned the C'tan's knowledge into a working process; and the population marched in under the orders of their monarch, the Silent King. The C'tan fed on the spiritual residue of an entire species as it was rendered down. The cost was the soul. The Necrontyr were gone, replaced by the soulless, undying Necrons. Most became near-mindless automatons; only nobles and specialists kept full personality. Reborn as machines and led by incarnate C'tan, the Necrons resumed the war and won, breaking the Old Ones. But the anguish poured into the Warp by that war helped twist it toward Chaos, a wound that outlasted every victory.

text: Two pieces of Necron technology define how they fight, and both flow from the same principle: matter and energy are theirs to command. Reanimation protocols are auto-repair systems woven through every Necron body. A wounded Necron knits itself back together in emerald sparks: reattaching limbs, reforming a shattered skull, witch-lights flaring back into dead eye-lenses. Damage too severe to repair on the spot triggers a phase-out, teleporting the mind and body to a tomb complex for rebuilding. There is a slow price. Each transference frays the engrams a little more, so a Necron that has died and reformed hundreds of times decays into a hollow automaton with no memory of who it was. Gauss weapons use the very same technology in reverse, breaking the molecular bonds of a target into raw energy that the Necrons can repurpose. A gauss flayer strips a victim apart layer by layer. On the tabletop these traits read as resilience and attrition: units claw wounds back at the end of a turn, and gauss fire shreds heavy armor.

text: Victory left the Necrons hollow and their empire in ruins, and a new power, the Aeldari, was rising. Rather than fight on, the Necrons turned on their C'tan masters; using a device called the Tesseract Labyrinth, they shattered the star-gods into shards and bound those fragments as weapons. Then they withdrew. Across thousands of Tomb Worlds, planets riddled with stasis crypts, the dynasties lay down to sleep, intending to wake after the galaxy's younger races had spent themselves. Szarekh, sick with guilt, freed his subjects from the command protocols that bound them to him and exiled himself into the void between galaxies. The Great Sleep was meant to last until the time was ripe. It did not go cleanly. Stasis decayed, dynasties woke damaged or feral, and by the 41st Millennium the Tomb Worlds are stirring across the galaxy, dynasty by dynasty, just as the Imperium reels from Chaos, Orks, Tyranids, and the rest.

section: Necron lords of the waking dynasties
text: The waking Necrons are not faceless. Szarekh, the Silent King, eventually returned from the intergalactic dark in the modern era, having seen a greater threat coming, and once more leads his people. Trazyn the Infinite is the obsessive archivist of the Necron race, a collector who pillages the galaxy for relics and living specimens, freezing famous warriors in stasis for his museum-world of Solemnace; on the battlefield he can snatch a fresh body when his own is destroyed. Imotekh the Stormlord, phaeron of the Sautekh Dynasty, is a brilliant and ruthless general who can call down lightning storms to blind and batter his foes before he strikes. Around them stand specialists like Orikan the Diviner, a chronomancer who reads and bends time, and Illuminor Szeras, the cold engineer of biotransference itself. Together they make the Necrons less a horde than a civilization: ancient, patient, and certain that the galaxy was always theirs to reclaim.

text:
Speedrunning is the practice of finishing a game as fast as possible under an agreed set of rules. Those rules are the whole game. A category defines what counts as the start, what counts as the end, and which techniques are legal in between. Most runs are RTA, timed by a real clock; a separate world of tool-assisted runs uses slowdown and frame-by-frame input to chart what is theoretically possible. The two inform each other. A TAS often proves a trick is real before any human lands it live.

The reason a game like Ocarina of Time rewards this is its depth. Many tricks are frame perfect, meaning the input has to land on a single frame out of sixty in a second. Others depend on the game's version, on the language set in the file name, or on the exact memory layout at one moment. Runners do not just play faster. They build a route, a fixed sequence of inputs and movement, and then practice that route until the hard parts become repeatable. The community keeps the routes, the glitch documentation, and the leaderboards in shared archives, mostly on speedrun.com and the older ZeldaSpeedRuns site.

text:
The category list is its own vocabulary. Any% finishes the game by any means; glitches are not only allowed, they are the point. 100% sits at the other end and requires everything the game tracks: every item, upgrade, and collectible. Between them sit categories that pick a different finish line or ban a class of trick.

Ocarina of Time's main extended categories include MST (Medallions, Stones, and Trials), which requires every spiritual stone and medallion, clearing the trials, and beating Ganon, and All Dungeons, which requires completing every area that contains a blue warp. All Dungeons and 100% are widely regarded as the hardest to run, the first for how brutally it punishes a mistake, the second for its length and near-perfect movement. Glitchless is its own family with Any%, 100%, and All Main Quests variants, governed by a long rulebook about what counts as a glitch. Several categories split further into SRM and No SRM versions, because the most powerful modern exploit changes the route so completely that running with and without it are almost different games.

text:
Two early tricks show how the routes grew from small exploits. Get-Item Manipulation is a fast way to obtain a bottle. Rather than open a chest and watch the animation, a runner triggers the game's item delay, moves next to the chest, then takes the item by swimming up for air or taking damage at the right moment. The bottle matters because it became the key that later warps depended on.

Bottle Adventure, often shortened to B-A, exploits how the game handles time travel between child and adult Link. The game stores which items sit on the B and C buttons for both ages so the player does not re-equip after traveling. By selecting an item on C-Right as a child and then pulling the Master Sword, a runner can place that item onto B, the slot normally reserved for the sword. The simplest payoff is using items where they are normally banned, such as bombs inside the Temple of Time. Its more dangerous cousin, Reverse Bottle Adventure (RBA), reverses the flow: a bottle on B writes values into inventory addresses, including memory just past the inventory, letting a runner flip the bits that represent songs, stones, and key items. RBA can grant items the runner never earned, at the cost of others.

text: The Infinite Sword Glitch (ISG) is one of the oldest building blocks in the kit. When it is active, Link's sword stays "on" without a swing, so anything that touches the blade takes damage continuously. There is a tradeoff: while in this state Link loses the ability to automatically hop off ledges, so he will not fall where he normally would. In the original game it is most often set up with a bomb, using the bomb as the object Link interacts with to trigger the state. ISG matters less for the damage and more as a foundation. It is used to set up several other tricks, including the bottle-based glitches, and it shows the pattern that runs through all of these exploits: a small, harmless-looking state that becomes a tool when chained into something larger.

text: Wrong Warp is the trick that first broke Any% wide open. The game numbers its areas, and a transition loaded while the game is confused about its destination can send Link to the wrong entry in that list. ZFG, then known as ZeldaFreakGlitcha, used this to set a world record in 2012 with the "Ganondoor" exploit, warping from the first dungeon toward the end of the game and cutting almost twelve minutes off the prior record, to a time reported around 34:59. The discovery that made this so powerful was a coincidence in the area numbering: the scene right after the Deku Tree is the collapsing-tower scene from Ganon's Castle, so a warp from the starting dungeon could leap most of the way to the finish. Earlier in the game's history, before this anything-goes era, the records authority Twin Galaxies banned glitches, so the first competitive runs were a fast version of the intended game. Cosmo (Cosmo Wright, later Narcissa Wright) was a prominent figure in that transition and pushed early glitched routes; one reported milestone is an 18:10 run in 2014. The exact attribution and timing of some early records are contested, so treat specific times as documented milestones rather than fixed facts.

section: Stale Reference Manipulation and the collapse
text:
The deepest break came from Stale Reference Manipulation (SRM), which entered the public route around late 2019. At its core SRM is a use-after-free bug. The game tracks actors (entities such as rocks, enemies, and items) in a heap, and some actors hold a reference to another actor they are carrying; Link holds a reference to a bush or rock above his head, the boomerang to whatever it grabbed. If that carried actor is unloaded while the reference survives, the game keeps writing position and rotation values to the freed memory. Load something new into that space and those writes corrupt it. Runners control what loads there through careful "heap manipulation."

What you can corrupt escalates in levels: editing an actor's data, then redirecting a function pointer such as the draw-function pointer to existing code, then calling code partway into a function. That last step reaches arbitrary code execution (ACE), where manipulated values become valid instructions and the game can be made to do almost anything, including jumping to the credits. The result was a fast collapse of the Any% record. As far as is documented, the route held near seventeen minutes for years, then a credits warp dropped it under thirteen minutes, then a later warp into ending cutscenes pushed the headline category under ten minutes, with reported times such as 9:56. ACE's power has been shown off in stunts like spawning Star Fox 64 Arwings on an unmodified cartridge. Because the route keeps changing, any single record number here is a snapshot; the community has long treated the game as something that keeps finding new ways to break.

text:
Majora's Mask is the sister discipline, built on a different engine quirk. Its signature exploit involves the Song of Soaring and a "hidden owl" in West Clock Town. If a runner has activated no other owl statue, playing the Song of Soaring has no listed destination and the game falls back to a default. The discovery, credited to Indextic in early 2014, is that the default is not fixed; it follows where the cursor last sat on the pause-menu map, and each owl point maps to a spot on that map. This index-warping lets a runner teleport widely, with counterintuitive results (aiming the cursor at one town can warp Link to a different road). A more advanced form overflows the text box that appears after the song, letting runners grab boss remains and the Fierce Deity's Mask and head for the final dungeon, even though it can require playing the song dozens of times. One reported milestone is ennopp112's record falling from 1:15:52 to 49:42 once the ocarina method matured.

Majora's Mask also runs Glitchless and No Major Glitches categories, and it shares foundational tricks with Ocarina of Time, including ISG. The three-day clock and the Song of Soaring as fast travel give it a routing flavor all its own, but the underlying pattern is identical to its older sibling: a beloved game taken apart, deliberately, by people who know it well enough to find the seams.
